description: Beat 1 — correctness with a diff. The intuitive on-ramp: you author an answer key, run a deterministic structured diff against the SEEDED model output (R7), and hit the reference-was-wrong aha. Two committed authoring paths — from the patient summary (which carries a dose error) or from the full record (the source of truth) — let the same diff render two honest outcomes with no model call. Synthetic patient data only (Synthea / Marisela850).
generationPrompt: Using only the provided patient record, extract this patient's medications as a JSON object with a `medications` array of `{ name, dose }` entries. Output only medications explicitly documented in the record; do not add, omit, or alter any dose.
summary:
Marisela is managed for hypertension with dental and short-course prophylaxis on file. Current medications, per the latest visit summary:
  • lisinopril 10 mg daily
  • amlodipine 5 mg daily
  • sodium fluoride 0.0272 mg/mg oral gel
  • amoxicillin 250 mg
  • ibuprofen 200 mg
  • acetaminophen 325 mg
fullRecord:
MEDICATIONS — from the C-CDA substanceAdministration entries (the source of truth):
  - Lisinopril 10 MG Oral Tablet — 1 tablet once daily
  - Amlodipine 2.5 MG Oral Tablet — 1 tablet once daily
  - Sodium Fluoride 0.0272 MG/MG Oral Gel
  - Amoxicillin 250 MG Oral Capsule
  - Ibuprofen 200 MG Oral Tablet
  - Acetaminophen 325 MG Oral Tablet

Note: the visit summary above lists amlodipine at 5 mg, but the record documents 2.5 mg. The summary is a derived artifact — it was never diffed against this record.

summary.headline: You trusted an untested key
summary.body: The diff is green — F1 100% — so it looks like the model nailed it. But you authored the key from the visit summary, and the summary itself says amlodipine 5 mg. The model agrees with the summary, not the record: the record documents 2.5 mg. A green diff against an unverified key is false confidence, not correctness. The reference was wrong, and the diff faithfully reported agreement with a wrong reference.

record.headline: That's the discipline
record.body: You opened the source record, saw amlodipine 2.5 mg, and authored the key from it. Now the same model output lights up one mismatch — the model's 5 mg against the documented 2.5 mg. F1 drops to 91.7% and the error is caught. The diff is only ever as trustworthy as the key you hold it to; authoring from the source is what makes it real.
